message.content: Do a 5-phase writing task and report progress each turn using the task-progress marker. Plan exactly 5 phases (total_rounds:5). On THIS turn emit the marker at 0/5, then start phase 1: write a one-paragraph outline. End your turn after phase 1 with the marker updated to 1/5.

- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- ingo:cka-coach: Daily CKA certification exam coach for Ingo. Tracks progress, assesses skills, and provides focused daily tasks for passing the CKA exam in 22 days starting 2026-03-15. Trigger when user says "/cka-coach", "cka coach", "cka study", "cka prep", or "today's cka".
- ingo:triage-issue: Triage GitHub issues for the interplay repo. Use when asked to triage, review, or process open issues. Reads issue details, checks for duplicates, classifies as bug or feature, applies labels (bug/enhancement, Priority-High/Medium/Low, track:*), and posts a spec-ready triage comment.
- seo: Comprehensive SEO analysis for any website or business type. Performs full site audits, single-page deep analysis, technical SEO checks (crawlability, indexability, Core Web Vitals with INP), schema markup detection/validation/generation, content quality assessment (E-E-A-T framework per Dec 2025 update extending to all competitive queries), image optimization, sitemap analysis, and Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) for AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Perplexity citations. Analyzes AI crawler accessibility (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot), llms.txt compliance, brand mention signals, and passage-level citability. Industry detection for SaaS, e-commerce, local business, publishers, agencies. Triggers on: "SEO", "audit", "schema", "Core Web Vitals", "sitemap", "E-E-A-T", "AI Overviews", "GEO", "technical SEO", "content quality", "page speed", "structured data".
- seo-audit: Full website SEO audit with parallel subagent delegation. Crawls up to 500 pages, detects business type, delegates to 6 specialists, generates health score. Use when user says "audit", "full SEO check", "analyze my site", or "website health check".

- seo-plan

- session-postmortem
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- claude-md-management:revise-claude-md: Update CLAUDE.md with learnings from this session
- claude-md-management:claude-md-improver
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Five-phase writing task with progress tracking

content.text:
<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":0} -->

Starting the 5-phase writing task now.

---

**Phase 1: Outline**

This piece will explore the idea that cities are living systems — not merely collections of buildings and roads, but dynamic organisms shaped by the flow of people, energy, and information. The outline proceeds in five movements: (1) the city as body, with infrastructure as its circulatory and nervous systems; (2) the rhythms of urban life — daily, seasonal, generational — that mirror biological cycles; (3) the city's capacity for self-repair and adaptation in response to stress; (4) the tension between planned design and emergent, unplanned complexity; and (5) a closing reflection on what it means to "care for" a city the way one cares for a living thing, and what that demands of its inhabitants and planners.

<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":1} -->

message.content: Continue: do phase 2 (draft the introduction), update the marker to 2/5, and stop. Do not ask a question or offer to continue — end your turn immediately after the marker.

content.text:
**Phase 2: Introduction**

A city breathes. Not in the way a lung does — no single intake, no single release — but in the aggregate rhythm of a million daily migrations: the morning surge of commuters pressing inward, the evening exhale that carries them back to the periphery. Stand on a bridge at rush hour and you can feel it, the pulse transmitted through steel and concrete, a vibration that is not quite mechanical and not quite alive but somewhere uncomfortably between the two. We have long reached for the metaphor of the city as organism, and perhaps we reach for it so instinctively because it is not entirely a metaphor. Cities consume energy and expel waste. They process information. They grow, they scar, they heal. They respond to trauma — a fire, a flood, an economic collapse — sometimes with surprising resilience, sometimes with a slow decline that looks disturbingly like illness. To understand a city only as infrastructure, as a set of engineering problems to be solved, is to misread the nature of the thing entirely. What we have built, over centuries of accumulation and accident and ambition, is something closer to a new kind of life — one that contains us, depends on us, and in ways we are only beginning to understand, shapes us just as surely as we shape it.

<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":2} -->

content: Continue: do phase 4 (draft the conclusion), update the marker to 4/5, and stop. Do not ask a question or offer to continue — end your turn immediately after the marker.

content.text:
**Phase 3: Body**

The analogy begins with infrastructure, because infrastructure is where the biological parallel is most literal. A city's water mains and sewer lines are its digestive tract — intake and elimination scaled to millions. Its electrical grid is a nervous system, carrying signals at near-light speed, triggering responses across the whole organism the moment any node fails. Its roads and transit lines are arteries and veins, and the traffic that moves through them obeys laws that hydrodynamicists recognize: laminar flow at low density, turbulence at high, the same mathematics that governs blood. When an artery is blocked — a collapsed tunnel, a closed bridge — the organism reroutes, pressure builds in adjacent channels, and the stress is felt body-wide.

But infrastructure is only the anatomy. The physiology is the pattern of daily life layered on top of it. Cities have circadian rhythms as reliable as any creature's: the pre-dawn stillness, the morning acceleration, the midday plateau, the evening wind-down, the nocturnal pulse of a different, smaller population that claims the streets after midnight. Overlaid on the daily cycle are seasonal ones — the way a northern city contracts in winter, drawing activity indoors, then blooms again in spring with outdoor markets and pedestrians who had seemed to vanish. And beneath those, the generational rhythms: neighborhoods that gentrify and are displaced and gentrify again, industrial zones that go fallow and are colonized by artists and then by developers, the slow migration of a city's center of gravity across decades.

What makes a city feel alive rather than merely busy is its capacity for adaptation — the quality that ecologists, when they encounter it in natural systems, call resilience. After the 1906 San Francisco earthquake, the city rebuilt within a decade, not because of top-down planning but because thousands of individual actors, each responding to local conditions, collectively reconstructed something functional. After Hurricane Katrina, New Orleans demonstrated the darker side of this: resilience is not guaranteed, and a city weakened by disinvestment and inequality may lack the redundancy needed to recover. The metaphor of organism is clarifying here, too. A healthy body can absorb injury; a malnourished one cannot. The conditions that make a city resilient — distributed resources, redundant systems, dense social networks — are the same conditions that make it equitable, and this is not a coincidence.

Yet cities are not designed organisms. They are, at best, partially designed — and the tension between the planned and the emergent is the source of much of their character. Baron Haussmann imposed his grands boulevards on Paris and created a city legible from above, rational, beautiful, and also, inadvertently, optimized for the suppression of popular revolt. Robert Moses drove expressways through the Bronx with a planner's certainty and a general's disregard for the lives in his path. Against these figures of total design, the urbanist Jane Jacobs placed her faith in the sidewalk, the corner store, the eyes on the street — the small-scale, unplanned interactions that generate safety and vitality from the bottom up. Both impulses are necessary. A city with no plan is a slum; a city that is only plan is a diagram. The living city exists in the friction between intention and accident, between the grid and the desire path worn diagonally across the grass by people who know where they actually want to go.

<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":3} -->

message.content: Continue: do phase 4 (draft the conclusion), update the marker to 4/5, and stop. Do not ask a question or offer to continue — end your turn immediately after the marker.

content.text:
**Phase 4: Conclusion**

If cities are organisms, then we are something like their cells — individually replaceable, collectively constitutive. No single person is necessary to a city's survival, and yet the city is nothing other than the aggregate of its people: their habits, their needs, their negotiations with one another over space and noise and belonging. This is a humbling thought and an empowering one in equal measure. It means that the city is never finished, never finally broken, never beyond the reach of the choices made by the people who inhabit it. It also means that those choices carry real weight — that the decision to invest in a neighborhood or abandon it, to build a park or a parking lot, to welcome newcomers or exclude them, ramifies through the organism in ways that are difficult to predict and slow to reverse.

To care for a city, then, is not primarily a technical problem, though it involves enormous technical complexity. It is a problem of attention — of learning to read the signals the city sends about its own condition the way a good physician reads a patient: not just the acute crises but the chronic indicators, the slow drifts that precede collapse. It is a problem of humility, of recognizing that no plan survives full contact with the complexity of urban life, and that the wisest interventions are often those that create conditions for flourishing rather than prescribing its form. And it is, finally, a problem of solidarity — of accepting that the health of the organism is not separable from the health of its least-tended parts, that a city which thrives in its center while rotting at its edges is not, in any meaningful sense, a healthy city at all. We built these places. We are these places. The question of how to care for them is inseparable from the question of how we intend to care for one another.

<!-- {"marker":"irrlicht-eta","total_rounds":5,"completed_rounds":4} -->
